EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An instability axis over southeast New Mexico, in combination with remnant outflow boundaries, resulted in isolated severe thunderstorms developing during the evening hours.
An instability axis over southeast New Mexico, in combination with remnant outflow boundaries, resulted in isolated severe thunderstorms developing during the evening hours.

A very large upper low moved off the Pacific and across the Rockies. Ahead of this system southwesterly flow brought significant moisture into the region. As the storm system and associated cold front moved slowly through southern New Mexico, snow levels dropped to around 5000 feet with almost 2 feet of snow reported near Cloudcroft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals across the Sacramento Mountains were 22.9 inches 1.8 miles southwest of Cloudcroft, 18.5 inches 5 miles northeast of Cloudcroft, and 6.5 inches 1 mile west of High Rolls at elevation 6601 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very large upper low moved off the Pacific and across the Rockies. Ahead of this system southwesterly flow brought significant moisture into the region. As the storm system and associated cold front moved slowly through southern New Mexico, snow levels dropped to around 5000 feet with almost 2 feet of snow reported near Cloudcroft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highest snowfall reported in zone was 9.9 inches 6.1 miles north of Silver City. Other reports included 9.0 inches 3.4 miles north of Silver City, 6.0 inches 3.5 south of Silver City and 5.5 inches 8.9 miles east-northeast of Bayard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cutoff low moved out of Arizona and across New Mexico with plenty of moisture in place from previous storm systems. Snow levels started out around 8000 feet and quickly fell behind strong cold front to below 5000 feet. Snowfall of up to 11 inches was reported in Cloudcroft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports across the zone included 9.0 inches 10 miles north of Central at the Signal Peak SNOTEL, 7.0 inches 10 miles northwest of Kingston at the McKnight Cabin SNOTEL, and 6.0 inches 9 miles east-northeast of Bayard from a CoCoRaHs observer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system blew through New Mexico on April Fool's Day. Nearly all areas east of the central mountain chain recorded wind gusts that reached or exceeded 58 mph.  Blowing dust reduced visibilities to around a mile in many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Raton ASOS measured sustained winds over 40 mph for about a four hour period. The highest sustained wind of 48 mph was recorded around 2 pm MST.

Widespread heavy rains fell across the northwest mountains during the early morning hours of the 17th, including an inch of rain that fell in Chama. The heavy rain on existing snow pack caused the Rio Chamita to flood, west of Chama.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rising water from the Rio Chamita resulted in flooding of yards, storage sheds and possibly a barn on the west side of Chama.  No homes were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad Pacific upper low moved across the desert southwest and southern New Mexico. A strong surface high over the central U.S. brought low level easterly winds into the region providing plenty of moisture and cold air.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow was reported across the Southern Sacramento Mountains with snowfall totals of up to 14.5 inches reported in Cloudcroft. Other reports included 13 inches 16 miles east of Cloudcroft, 10 inches in Sacramento and Dunken, 8 inches near Elk and 7.2 inches in Sunspot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper low digging out of the Pacific Northwest brought a strong cold front with damaging winds gusts of up to 76 mph to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station located 1 mile west of La Luz, NM reported a peak wind gust of 69 mph.  Other wind gusts reported included: 61 mph at High Rolls and 58 mph two miles north of Alamogordo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper low digging out of the Pacific Northwest brought a strong cold front with damaging winds gusts of up to 76 mph to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Truth or Consequences(KTCS) ASOS reported a peak wind gust of 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper low digging out of the Pacific Northwest brought a strong cold front with damaging winds gusts of up to 76 mph to the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 76 mph was reported at the Dripping Springs RAWS.  The National Weather Service Office in Santa Teresa, NM recorded a gust to 59 mph and the Las Cruces Airport(KLRU) reported a gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms were the result of a back door cold front which slid through the eastern plains of New Mexico during the day.  Initially, thunderstorms brought hail and gusty winds across southeast New Mexico.  Then later, the thunderstorms evolved into a cluster which slowly moved east into Texas.  This cluster of storms brought rainfall amounts of up to 2 inches in one hours time across the east.  Later that night, the front pushed through the gaps of the central mountain chain resulting in east winds topping 60 mph.  Tree damage was noted across much of Albuquerque.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Plenty of moisture remained across eastern New Mexico behind the previous days back door cold front. Thunderstorms with heavy rain pounded western San Miguel County, resulting in a flash flood in Bernal, knocking out bridges on county roads B27 and B28.

A back door cold front that burst through the gaps of the central mountains into the Lower Rio Grande Valley brought increased moisture to the area. A weak disturbance aloft added the instability to trigger a round of thunderstorms, one of which brought nickel size hail to Socorro County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail accumulated to a depth of 3 inches and ranged in size from peas to nickels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled over the eastern plains, along with an approaching trough, brought moisture, lift and instability to far eastern New Mexico on the 12th. Numerous large hail was observed along with a few high wind reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated near 50 mph damaged a 15,000 square foot tent at The Downs of Santa Fe which housed the flea market.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving upper level storm system and its associated cold front brought very strong winds to the state on the 10th.  Strong winds started early in the morning, with most locations gusting near 60 mph before noon.  The strong winds created areas of blowing dust with reduced visibility down to 2 miles or less at times.  The gusty winds also took down power poles, broke tree limbs and tore a roof off a mobile home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter at Sumner Lake recorded a peak wind gust of 65 mph.  Visibility was reduced to 3 miles in blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lone severe storm moved east along the Chaves/Eddy county line during the early evening hours, dropping quarter size hail along SR 13.  The hail covered the road, as reported by Vortex 2 probe team.

A diffuse dryline remained over the eastern plains on the afternoon and evening of the 18th.  Storms struggled to form until late in the afternoon, but still did not reach their full potential until they moved east into Texas. Nonetheless, penny to nickel sized hail was reported with a few storms.

The gusty winds also took down power poles, broke tree limbs and tore a roof off a mobile home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cannon AFB ASOS recorded sustained winds above 40 mph for an hour.  The highest sustained wind speed recorded was 45 mph.

A large complex of showers and thunderstorms slowly moved eastward across the eastern plains of New Mexico during the early morning hours of the 16th.  Rainfall amounts around 2 inches were common, with isolated areas receiving up to 5 inches.  The flooding closed many roadways around Roswell and Portales.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple roads were flooded across northern Roosevelt County, particularly around Floyd and Portales. One citizen attempted to drive through a low water crossing filled with three to four feet of water along Highway 267 near Floyd.  The car stalled, but was recovered.  18th Street in Portales was a particularly hard hit area.  Streets along 18th were impassible between Avenue D and Globe.  The intersection of Avenue I and 18th Street was impassible as well.  The cooperative observer in Portales measured 1.75 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a line of showers and thunderstorms moved east into more moisture, they increased in intensity and began to train over each other. Portions of southwest Quay County received nearly 4 inches of rain, which made the creeks run high.  A low water crossing was flooded out just north of Quay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low water crossing along Quay Road 49 flooded out due to the heavy rains. This area only received about an inch of rain, however, areas upstream received nearly 4 inches, resulting in high water in all area creeks and streams.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture combined with an upper level wave sliding north across western New Mexico produced more torrential rainfall and repeat flooding for areas around Gallup and the Jemez Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Chavez Road and Woodview Drive in Baca as well as Navajo Road in Thoreau were washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Residual monsoon moisture combined with a slow moving trough over southern New Mexico to produce slow moving, heavy rain producing thunderstorms over the Gila.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A truck was swept into creek near Highway 180 and mile marker 82.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moderately moist and unstable environment under an upper ridge provided for a pulse severe storm to develop over Las Cruces, NM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch hail was reported 3 miles northwest of Las Cruces and also 2 miles northeast of Las Cruces.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level disturbance moved through southern New Mexico in a modestly moist and unstable environment. A weak surface trough was also present which helped aid in thunderstorm development over the lower elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 8' by 5' deep storm drainage ditch was overrunning onto the adjacent street in the south part of Hurley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level disturbance moved through southern New Mexico in a modestly moist and unstable environment. A weak surface trough was also present which helped aid in thunderstorm development over the lower elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated wind gust of 60 mph was reported with blowing dust 2 miles north of Columbus.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level disturbance moved through southern New Mexico in a modestly moist and unstable environment. A weak surface trough was also present which helped aid in thunderstorm development over the lower elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was occurring on New Mexico Highway 11 between mile markers 7 and 9. Barricades were put up to stop traffic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level disturbance moved through southern New Mexico in a modestly moist and unstable environment. A weak surface trough was also present which helped aid in thunderstorm development over the lower elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor field flooding around residence after multiple storms moved across area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture remained in the area during the evening hours of the 2nd and into the morning hours of the 3rd. Heavy rains from prolific lightning producing storms moved across the Santa Fe area for the second night in a row, resulting in flooding of many roadways.  Many locations picked up another one to two inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple roads closed due to at least 10 inches of water flowing over the road.  This included the low water crossing at Dawn Trail and Arroyo Trail, and also at Turquoise Trail and County Road 45.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture and some low level convergence helped spark thunderstorms over the eastern plains. A few of these storms turned severe with damaging wind gusts and large hail.
Thunderstorms moving off the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo mountains strengthened as they moved into the Raton area, producing large hail and high wind gusts.
Thunderstorms moving off the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo mountains strengthened as they moved into the Raton area, producing large hail and high wind gusts.
A back door cold front delivered even more moisture to eastern New Mexico, resulting in thunderstorms producing heavy rain and high wind gusts, along with penny size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to flooding from Chavez Lake, 14th Street was closed between N. Edwards and N. Hull streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoon moisture combined with an easterly wave over southeast New Mexico to create widespread heavy rain across northern and central New Mexico. Hardest hit areas were in the vicinity of the central mountain chain, where several flash floods were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple dirt roads were washed out in an area called Tramperos.  Vehicles were stuck in the mud and high water.  The Bartley RAWS near Gascon received over 2 inches of rain in less than 2 hours time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low level moisture and an approaching cold front teamed together to produce a couple of strong thunderstorms.
Low level moisture and an approaching cold front teamed together to produce a couple of strong thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail lasted 15 minutes and accumulated four to five inches deep. The hail broke a car windshield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture remained in the area during the evening hours of the 2nd and into the morning hours of the 3rd. Heavy rains from prolific lightning producing storms moved across the Santa Fe area for the second night in a row, resulting in flooding of many roadways.  Many locations picked up another one to two inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the satellite dish on top of a home southwest of Tesuque.  The current traveled down a PVC pipe into the house and blew out a 1 by 2 foot area of sheet rock.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture remained in the area during the evening hours of the 2nd and into the morning hours of the 3rd. Heavy rains from prolific lightning producing storms moved across the Santa Fe area for the second night in a row, resulting in flooding of many roadways.  Many locations picked up another one to two inches of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The intersection of Los Pinos and Sunrise in La Cienega was not passable due to high water and the road was closed for a time.  A nearby arroyo had debris in it with fast moving water about 6 feet high.  A backhoe had been left in the arroyo somewhat downstream the previous day.  This piece of equipment was almost entirely enveloped in water and mud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep southerly flow provided a deep layer of monsoon moisture to the region with|slow moving storms. The high moisture content and slow storm motion contributed to the heavy rain and flash flooding in Dona Ana County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported going over West Canal Road Bridge in Hatch. In the Rincon/Radium Springs area near Santiago Peak Road and Highway 185 flooding pushed cars off of road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A short wave trough approaching New Mexico brought moisture and instability to the far northwest portion of the state, resulting in a report of large hail northeast of Farmington.

A strong upper level low pressure system located over northern Baja California ushered in increased moisture, instability and shear across western New Mexico.  A few storms produced up to quarter size hail.

A strong upper level low pressure system located over northern Baja California ushered in increased moisture, instability and shear across western New Mexico.  A few storms produced up to quarter size hail.

The same upper low pressure system that resulted in hail across western New Mexico on the 20th, neared and moved over the state on the 21st.  Two storms turned severe with better moisture and shear to work with. One storm, over the northeast heights of Albuquerque, dropped up to quarter size hail.  Then later, a lone, slow moving storm over central Chaves County, produced two tornadoes.  Fortunately, these tornadoes occurred over rural areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was reported at both San Mateo and I-25 as well as Academy and Burlison.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported near the intersection of Spain and Tramway in Albuquerque.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Easterly winds brought deep moisture into the region. A surface trough extending through the Rio Grande Valley provided the lift for strong to severe thunderstorms to develop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: El Paso Electric reported 11 power poles were knocked down causing 11,000 people to be without power for some time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An impressive storm system moved across northern Baja California on the 27th and crossed New Mexico on the 28th. Plenty of moisture was drawn north into the state, while a strong back door cold front brought chilly air to the eastern plains. This combination resulted in heavy snow from the central mountain chain across the eastern plains to the Texas border. Rain changed to freezing rain and sleet before turning to all snow over portions of Chaves, Roosevelt and Curry counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall over the Jemez mountains averaged four to eight inches, with locally up to 16 inches over the high peaks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper ridge was centered over the Four Corners area with deep easterly flow across Southern New Mexico allowing for heavy rains to develop over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall totals of up to 2.00 inches were reported in Radium Springs with several inches of standing water on streets and low lying areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak disturbance moving through west-southwest flow aloft provided lift for thunderstorms over Dona Ana County. Dew points in the 40s at the surface combined with temperatures in the 90s allowed for severe winds to be produced with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated up to 65 MPH in northeast Las Cruces.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level trough, combined with a back door cold front and abundant moisture, set the stage for heavy snow across much of central and eastern New Mexico. The front quickly moved across the plains and easterly upslope flow behind the front helped focus the heaviest snow amounts along the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo and Sandia Mountains.  The front pushed through the gaps of the central mountain chain near Santa Fe, allowing significant snows to develop over the capital city just after morning rush hour on the 16th. However, farther south, around the Albuquerque Metro area, the front was delayed, and the precipitation remained as rain until the evening hours. The snow created multiple traffic problems, and portions of both I-40 and  I-25 were closed for a time due to hazardous road conditions and multiple accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 4 and 7 inches of snow fell around the Taos area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Santa Fe Metro area was one of the hardest hit areas from this winter storm.  6 to 10 inches of snow was common in Santa Fe, however further south near Lamy, Madrid and Gallisteo, up to 22 inches of snow was measured. Interstate 25 was closed for a time from Santa Fe northward to Las Vegas due to treacherous driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A brief warm up after several rounds of snowfall across the Chuska Mountains led to melting snow and muddy roads.  A woman died of exposure after her vehicle got stuck in the mud and she tried to continue on foot.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An 88 year old woman was found dead 7 miles north of Crystal.  The woman insisted on visiting her sister at 4 am, but her vehicle got stuck in melting snow and mud. She tried to continue on foot, but was unsuccessful.  The woman's body was discovered several yards from her vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds and heavy snow plagued much of western and central New Mexico on the 29th and 30th.  The heavy snow began on the 29th across northwest New Mexico as a very large and cold upper level system slid southward across the Great Basin. Several inches of snow quickly accumulated across the Northwest Plateau.  After a brief lull on the night of the 29th, snow quickly picked back up along and behind a cold front that swept from west to east. Strong winds both ahead of and behind the front created areas of blowing snow and blizzard conditions across the southwest and south central mountains.  Though little or no precipitation occurred across the southeast, high winds were noted along and behind the front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over a foot and a half of snow fell across portions of the far northwest highlands. One foot was reported north of La Plata along the Colorado border, while 8 inches was reported at Navajo Dam.  Meanwhile, Dulce picked up close to 21 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds plagued the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains for much of the day.  The extended nature of the high winds downed several large cottonwood trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter in Sapello measured a 61 mph wind gust early in the morning, though the high winds continued through the afternoon hours.  Winds of at least 58 mph around Ute Park caused large cottonwood trees to blow down. 58 mph wind gusts were also recorded at the Angel Fire AWOS between 210pm and 330pm.

After a brief lull on the night of the 29th, snow quickly picked back up along and behind a cold front that swept from west to east. Strong winds both ahead of and behind the front created areas of blowing snow and blizzard conditions across the southwest and south central mountains.  Though little or no precipitation occurred across the southeast, high winds were noted along and behind the front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Eight Mile Draw RAWS site recorded a 58 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level trough, combined with a back door cold front and abundant moisture, set the stage for heavy snow across much of central and eastern New Mexico. The front quickly moved across the plains and easterly upslope flow behind the front helped focus the heaviest snow amounts along the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo and Sandia Mountains.  The front pushed through the gaps of the central mountain chain near Santa Fe, allowing significant snows to develop over the capital city just after morning rush hour on the 16th. However, farther south, around the Albuquerque Metro area, the front was delayed, and the precipitation remained as rain until the evening hours. The snow created multiple traffic problems, and portions of both I-40 and  I-25 were closed for a time due to hazardous road conditions and multiple accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Southwest of Corona, around 5 inches of snow fell. The snow packed roads caused a passenger bus to lose control on U.S. 54 15 miles south of Corona at 745 am local time.  The bus collided with a cattle truck and overturned. The bus driver and 19 passengers were hospitalized, some with critical injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The active weather pattern continued through early February with the 6th storm in three weeks impacted New Mexico. This storm crossed the central portion of the state and provided the best dynamics and moisture from Interstate 40 north to the Colorado border. Eight to 16 inches of snow was common at the ski areas and SNOTEL sites, with generally under eight inches at lower elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to five inches of snow fell across the Santa Fe Metro Area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An impressive storm system moved across northern Baja California on the 27th and crossed New Mexico on the 28th. Plenty of moisture was drawn north into the state, while a strong back door cold front brought chilly air to the eastern plains. This combination resulted in heavy snow from the central mountain chain across the eastern plains to the Texas border. Rain changed to freezing rain and sleet before turning to all snow over portions of Chaves, Roosevelt and Curry counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to nine inches of snow fell over the high terrain of the southern Sangre de Cristo mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first in a series of disturbances crossed northern New Mexico on the evening of the 18th through mid afternoon on the 19th.  An east-west band of lower elevation rain and higher elevation snow developed and slowly moved north across western and central New Mexico overnight. Over eight inches of snow accumulated across the higher terrain, while amounts of 2 to 6 inches were common across the western and northern valleys.  The hardest hit areas stretched from the Jemez Mountains, eastward across the Santa Fe Metro Area, to the southern Sangre De Cristo Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 9 inches of snow fell across the highest elevations of the Jemez Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third disturbance within a broad trough brought the first significant snowfall to northwest New Mexico.  However, most of the snowfall fell above 7500 feet, and amounts over 8 inches were confined to elevations above 9000 feet.  Nonetheless, due to the below normal snowpack so far this season, the snowfall was welcomed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hopewell SNOTEL at 10,000 feet picked up about 8 inches of snow.  Around Chama, 4 to 6 inches was common.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A man died from exposure in a vacant lot in Gallup.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds aloft in response to the approach of an upper level system coupled with a tight surface pressure gradient led to windy conditions near Ruidoso and Las Vegas.  Winds speeds ramped up by mid morning and continued through much of the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Las Vegas ASOS peaked at 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front associated with the last in a series of upper level troughs, sent a blast of blustery conditions down the eastern plains of New Mexico.  Hardest hit was the Clayton area, where high winds and light snow continued for several hours.

A cold front associated with the last in a series of upper level troughs, sent a blast of blustery conditions down the eastern plains of New Mexico.  Hardest hit was the Clayton area, where high winds and light snow continued for several hours.

A winter storm dropped several inches of snow across the far northwest highlands and San Juan Mountains of New Mexico.  SNOTELS in the region reported up to 8 inches of new snow. Meanwhile, 4 to 7 inches of snow were reported by local cooperative observers.  The storm also dropped snow across the Sangre de Cristo Mountains and central high plains, though amounts were not quite as high.  Roadways across northwest New Mexico became snow-packed and icy including Highway 64/84 between Chama and Dulce.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Though most of the far northwest highlands only received an inch or two of snow, the higher elevation locations picked up significantly higher amounts. The cooperative observer at Dulce reported 7 inches of fresh snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed underneath a band of higher mid-level moisture.  In addition, very moist conditions near the surface resulted in modest surface based instability across southeast New Mexico.  Although the environment was not favored for tornado development, a very brief landspout was observed in Eddy County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief land spout tornado developed on the north end of Artesia, NM.  The exact location of this tornado was estimated according to the spotter's description.  No damage was done, and it only persisted for about 1 minute.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Both the Hopewell and Chamita SNOTELS picked up about 8 inches of new snow.  The cooperative observer in Chama only picked up 4 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds and heavy snow plagued much of western and central New Mexico on the 29th and 30th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level trough, combined with a back door cold front and abundant moisture, set the stage for heavy snow across much of central and eastern New Mexico. The front quickly moved across the plains and easterly upslope flow behind the front helped focus the heaviest snow amounts along the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo and Sandia Mountains.  The front pushed through the gaps of the central mountain chain near Santa Fe, allowing significant snows to develop over the capital city just after morning rush hour on the 16th. However, farther south, around the Albuquerque Metro area, the front was delayed, and the precipitation remained as rain until the evening hours. The snow created multiple traffic problems, and portions of both I-40 and  I-25 were closed for a time due to hazardous road conditions and multiple accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The west side of Albuquerque northeastward through Rio Rancho and Placitas were the hardest hit areas. 3 to 7 inches of snow quickly accumulated on roads and grassy surfaces.  The Albuquerque valley, northeast heights and foothills had less than an inch of snow.  Most of the precipitation in these areas stayed as rain.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight to 16 inches of snow was common over the Sandia Mountains, with many areas reporting around a foot exactly. However, Sandia Ski Area reported a whopping 28 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds gusting as high as 74 mph plagued the Sangre de Cristo, Sandia and Manzano Mountains as well as their adjacent eastern highlands during the late morning hours on the 15th. Gusts near or just above 60 mph tore solar panels off the roof of a house southwest of Las Vegas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clines Corners ASOS recorded a peak wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Winds gusting as high as 74 mph plagued the Sangre de Cristo, Sandia and Manzano Mountains as well as their adjacent eastern highlands during the late morning hours on the 15th. Gusts near or just above 60 mph tore solar panels off the roof of a house southwest of Las Vegas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Las Vegas ASOS recorded a peak wind gust of 62 mph, though was gusting over 58 mph for over 30 minutes.  These strong winds blew 2 solar panels off the roof of a house on Highway 84 between mile markers 100 and 101 southwest of Las Vegas.  Meanwhile, a roof was blown off a vacant house near K-Bob's restaurant and also a tree was blown over on Columbia Avenue which blocked the street for a time.  Power outages were abundant across the region.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A grass fire started 14 miles southwest of Elida during the afternoon of the 15th.  Strong winds flamed the fire as it scorched approximately 4500 acres of grass and wheat fields.  One barn was destroyed.  The cause of the fire is unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper low digging out of the Pacific Northwest brought a strong cold front with damaging winds and heavy mountain snows to the region. Blizzard conditions were reported in many areas of Northern Grant County and the Sacramento Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions were reported at many locations around the Gila along with heavy snow. There were numerous reports of over 6 inches of snow across this region.  The heaviest snowfall totals that were reported include: 10.5 inches in Pinos Altos, 10.3 inches 4 miles south of Silver City, and 10.1 inches 2 miles north-northwest of Little Walnut Village.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper low digging out of the Pacific Northwest brought a strong cold front with damaging winds and heavy mountain snows to the region. Blizzard conditions were reported in many areas of Northern Grant County and the Sacramento Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow was reported in the Southern Sacramento Mountains with a maximum snowfall of 12 inches reported in Dry Canyon.  Other snowfall reports included 5.5 inches in Cloudcroft and 4.2 inches 2 miles south of Cloudcroft. Blizzard conditions were also reported in open areas across the Sacramento Mountains with a peak wind gust of |61 mph reported near High Rolls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storm systems brought heavy snow to much of the state between the 20th and the 22nd of February.  Light to moderate snows began across northwest New Mexico on the evening of the 20th.  However, as stronger systems passed over the state, snow amounts began to increase on the 21st and 22nd.  Over a foot of snow was noted in many mountain locales, while low lying areas generally received between 2 and 8 inches of snow.  By the afternoon of the 22nd, the eastern plains started to receive snow, which continued into the early morning hours of the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 10 inches of snow was reported around Mogollon and Quemado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight surface pressure gradient and surface pressure rises near or above 1 hpa per hour resulted in strong northeast winds across portions of the southeast New Mexico plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was also one gust of 59 MPH at 0000 LST 1/7/10.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level storm moved into southern California, passed just south of Las Vegas, then crossed central Arizona and New Mexico. Large scale lift and mid level forcing, along with low level moisture, resulted in widespread light to moderate snow from roughly interstate 40 north to the Colorado Border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around a foot of snow fell across the highest elevations of the South Central Mountains, including at Ski Apache.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As winter was officially drawing to a close, yet another blast of snow impacted New Mexico, primarily over the central mountains and adjacent eastern high plains. The storm responsible for the late winter snow plunged south from the Northern Rockies into the state early on the 20th. Snow began accumulating early on the 19th though, and continued into the morning of the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall over the Sandia and Manzano Mountains averaged seven to 13 inches.

By the afternoon of the 22nd, the eastern plains started to receive snow, which continued into the early morning hours of the 23rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As two upper level storm systems merged over New Mexico and moved into the Oklahoma and Texas Panhandles, wrap around precipitation once again left northeast New Mexico with several inches of snow on the ground.  Strong and gusty winds also resulted in blowing and drifting snow.  Snow drifts up to four feet were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Taos Ski Area picked up another foot of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing upper level trough and associated strong mid level winds mixing to the surface combined with a tight surface pressure gradient over the area to produce high winds across portions of the Guadalupe Mountains in New Mexico and the adjacent plains.

A strong closed upper low pressure system slid south across western New Mexico while dragging a strong back door cold front across the eastern plains.  The northern mountains as well as the east slopes of the central mountain chain were hit the hardest - with some locations receiving up to a foot of snow. The snow was responsible for several road closures, including portions of Interstate 25 and Interstate 40.  State Highway 475, also known as Hyde Park Road, was closed by State Police which stranded travelers returning from a day of skiing at Santa Fe Ski Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 8 inches of snow fell near Anton Chico.  Elsewhere, between 1 and 4 inches of snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system blew through New Mexico on April Fool's Day. Nearly all areas east of the central mountain chain recorded wind gusts that reached or exceeded 58 mph.  Blowing dust reduced visibilities to around a mile in many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near Mills, wind gusts were estimated to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It didn't take long for the spring winds to arrive once the season officially started.  Strong west to southwest winds blew across much of the state beginning during the early afternoon hours of the 26th.  Though winds calmed down overnight across the west, winds switched around to the north and northwest and continued to blow across the east overnight and into the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dunken RAWS site recorded sustained westerly wind speeds over 40 mph for at least 2 hours.  The peak sustained wind was 43 mph. The strong and gusty winds pealed a metal roof off an old barn east of Elk.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It didn't take long for the spring winds to arrive once the season officially started.  Strong west to southwest winds blew across much of the state beginning during the early afternoon hours of the 26th.  Though winds calmed down overnight across the west, winds switched around to the north and northwest and continued to blow across the east overnight and into the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Double Eagle Airport AWOS on the west side of the Albuquerque Metro Area recorded a peak wind gust of 59 mph.  Visibility was reduced down to 5 miles in blowing dust.  The strong winds collapsed a large tent sending a teenage boy to the hospital with minor injuries at the annual Pork and Brew in Rio Rancho.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold upper low moving out of the Pacific Northwest combined with a back door cold front to bring heavy snow to the Southern Sacramento Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum snowfall reported in the Southern Sacramento Mountains was 11.4 inches 1.8 miles southwest of Cloudcroft. Other reports across the zone included 9.2 inches in Cloudcroft, 8.1 inches 4.9 miles northeast of Cloudcroft and 7.3 inches in Sunspot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture spread from south to north across the state as an upper level low pressure system tracked from the boot-heel of New Mexico northeastward into the Texas panhandle.  Snow prevailed at higher elevations, however, precipitation changed from rain to snow during the late morning hours across lower elevations. The heaviest snow amounts stretched from the Sandia and Manzano Mountains, north and northeastward across the northeast plains, where colder temperatures were already in place. The snow resulted in over 50 traffic accidents around Las Vegas on the 3rd. One accident resulted in the death of a 39-year old deputy warden for the state Penitentiary. He was a passenger in a truck that was northbound on state road 3 near Ribera around 1230 pm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 3 and 8 inches of snow fell across Quay County.  The largest snow totals were found across northern portions of the county.  Around 6 inches fell in Tucumcari.

The heaviest snow amounts stretched from the Sandia and Manzano Mountains, north and northeastward across the northeast plains, where colder temperatures were already in place. He was a passenger in a truck that was northbound on state road 3 near Ribera around 1230 pm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 11 inches of snow was reported across Harding County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of storm systems brought heavy snow to much of the state between the 20th and the 22nd of February.  Light to moderate snows began across northwest New Mexico on the evening of the 20th.  However, as stronger systems passed over the state, snow amounts began to increase on the 21st and 22nd.  Over a foot of snow was noted in many mountain locales, while low lying areas generally received between 2 and 8 inches of snow.  By the afternoon of the 22nd, the eastern plains started to receive snow, which continued into the early morning hours of the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The west slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains picked up between 6 and 13 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sharp dry line moved west out of west Texas and became stationary over the far eastern plains on Sunday, May 23, 2010.  Meanwhile, an unseasonably deep upper level low pressure system over the Great Basin was moving slowly east toward New Mexico.  The combination of the upper level disturbance approaching New Mexico and extreme instability along the dry line set the stage for widespread severe weather from Quay County northeast into Union County.

A quick moving upper level storm system and its associated cold front brought very strong winds to the state on the 10th.  Strong winds started early in the morning, with most locations gusting near 60 mph before noon.  The strong winds created areas of blowing dust with reduced visibility down to 2 miles or less at times.  The gusty winds also took down power poles, broke tree limbs and tore a roof off a mobile home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Texas Mesonet site near Dora recorded peak wind gusts of 60 mph at 1035 am, 61 mph at 1130 am, and 59 mph at 1125 am MST. The high winds caused extensive damage to power polls in Portales, along with a few downed trees and part of a roof was blown off a barn.

A sharp dry line moved west out of west Texas and became stationary over the far eastern plains on Sunday, May 23, 2010.  Meanwhile, an unseasonably deep upper level low pressure system over the Great Basin was moving slowly east toward New Mexico.  The combination of the upper level disturbance approaching New Mexico and extreme instability along the dry line set the stage for widespread severe weather from Quay County northeast into Union County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong mid level winds preceding the arrival of an upper air trough resulted in strong winds across southeast New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended period of gusts over 60 mph was observed throughout the day.  The strongest gust occurred at 1350 MST, which was 69 mph.  Several structures were damaged by the winds including a chimney chase, home siding, a wooden fence, and 3 barns.  The roofs and siding on the barns were blown off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increased moisture ahead of a short wave trough over the Great Basin streamed into central and eastern New Mexico. This helped to produce isolated showers and thunderstorms, one of which turned severe near San Patricio.

An upper level trough of low pressure and surface cold front increased instability and lift across northeast New Mexico, with plenty of low level moisture available. This led to a few reports of large hail over San Miguel and Union Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranged from quarter to golf ball size and lasted from 525 pm to 535 pm MST.

A strong storm system moved into Arizona on the 22nd and re-organized over Southeast Colorado on the 23rd. Isolated high winds were recorded east and south of the storm, near Clines Corners and Ruidoso.

With abundant low level moisture in place just east of the dryline, afternoon heating led to the development of scattered thunderstorms over Eddy County.  These storms continued into the evening hours, with some storms reaching severe levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A high water rescue was executed along Armstrong Road near Walnut Arroyo.  The man was washed downstream 300 yards.  The rescue attempt was successful. No injuries were reported with this event, and the damage estimate was based on damage done to the vehicle.

An approaching upper level trough of low pressure and a surface cold front combined to cause several reports of high winds from Clines Corners to Las Vegas and Raton, east to Clayton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High sustained winds of 40 to 44 mph were reported by the Clayton ASOS between 1055 am and 355 pm MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching upper level trough of low pressure and a surface cold front combined to cause several reports of high winds from Clines Corners to Las Vegas and Raton, east to Clayton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 58 mph was also recorded at 1230 pm MST. High sustained winds of 40 mph occurred between 1055 am and 1155 am MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching upper level trough tightened the mid level pressure gradient and helped to produce a few high wind gusts around Gallup.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 58 mph was recorded at the Gallup ASOS at 223pm and 427pm MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry line over southeast New Mexico set up a boundary for an isolated severe thunderstorm near Hagerman in southeast Chaves County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel sized hail was reported at 630pm MST and quickly grew to Quarter size hail and continued until 740pm MST. The hail covered the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving, deep trough of low pressure was over Arizona on the 29th. High winds developed ahead of this system for the second day in a row, this time impacting a much greater area of northern and central New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated to be around 60 mph overturned two semi-trucks along U.S. 84 in Guadalupe county.  Both trucks were empty, which made them more susceptible to the strong wind gusts.  Luckily, no one was injured.   These gusts also knocked down an awning at Travel Centers of America.  Debris from the awning smashed a vehicle's window.   Elsewhere around the county, streets were littered with shingles blown off of rooftops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching upper level trough of low pressure and a surface cold front combined to cause several reports of high winds from Clines Corners to Las Vegas and Raton, east to Clayton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 71 mph also occurred at 135 pm MST. High wind gusts were recorded between 1023 am and 215 pm MST at speeds of 58 to 71 mph. High sustained winds of 51 mph occurred between 1153 am and 1253 pm MST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 4:45 PM MST, the public reported 12 to 18 inches of flowing water across their driveway and street in front of their home.  The flood waters destroyed around 7 acres of alfalfa crop.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front associated with a digging trough moving out of the Great Basin swept across the region producing high winds across much of southern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind of 50 knots was reported at the Silver City AWOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front associated with a digging trough moving out of the Great Basin swept across the region producing high winds across much of southern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak gust of 52 knots was reported at the Deming Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front associated with a digging trough moving out of the Great Basin swept across the region producing high winds across much of southern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak gust of 96 MPH was reported at a Mesonet site 1 mile southeast of San Augustin Pass in Dona Ana County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving upper level storm system and its associated cold front brought very strong winds to the state on the 10th.  Strong winds started early in the morning, with most locations gusting near 60 mph before noon.  The strong winds created areas of blowing dust with reduced visibility down to 2 miles or less at times.  The gusty winds also took down power poles, broke tree limbs and tore a roof off a mobile home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A School Net site near Ute Lake recorded a peak wind gust of 71 mph at 1027 am MST.  Report was relayed by KVII television in Amarillo.  Additionally, the ASOS in Tucumcari recorded a peak wind gust of 63 mph at 1038 am MST. However, wind gust over 58 mph were recorded between 908 and 1101 am.  A few powerlines were downed northeast of Logan which started a small grass fire.  Fortunately, the fire was quickly put out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist southeasterly low level flow coupled with an approaching upper level trough allowed numerous showers and thunderstorms to develop across eastern New Mexico.  Storms began early in the morning, where hail and even a tornado was reported across the southeast. Storms continued into the afternoon, where up to golf ball size hail was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds estimated around 85 mph uprooted at least a dozen mature pinon pine trees at 6340 feet in the northern Sacramento Mountains. In addition, a 500-gallon diesel tank that was estimated to be half full was lofted vertically and carried at least 150 yards, and an empty 53 foot box trailed was tipped on its side.  There was also minor damage to a horse/tack barn and a fire department truck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist southeasterly low level flow coupled with an approaching upper level trough allowed numerous showers and thunderstorms to develop across eastern New Mexico.  Storms began early in the morning, where hail and even a tornado was reported across the southeast. Storms continued into the afternoon, where up to golf ball size hail was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were downed along Forest Roads 376 and 126 blocking the roadway.  Pea size hail was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front associated with a digging trough moving out of the Great Basin swept across the region producing high winds across much of southern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak gust reported across the area was 75 MPH at Holloman Air Force Base Headquarters. An observation from 23 miles west of the Holloman Air Force Base observing site reported a peak gust of 71 MPH.  Other observations included 53 knots at the Holloman Air Force Base observing site, 59 MPH 4 miles southwest of the Holloman observing site, and 71 MPH 10 miles north-northeast of Holloman Air Force Base Headquarters.

A passing upper level shortwave trough resulted in strong winds in the Guadalupe and Davis Mountains.  Portions of the southeast New Mexico plains were also affected by these stronger winds on 1/22/10 and 1/23/10.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A sustained northwest wind of 46 MPH and gust of 59 MPH occurred at Artesia.  Virga showers and a dry subcloud layer likely enhanced the wind speeds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm off the West Coast moved inland on the 22nd, increasing the pressure gradient in the mid levels of the atmosphere. These winds mixed down to the surface, producing high wind gusts over the south central mountains and eastern plains.

The third and final storm during the week of January 18th delivered heavy snow to much of western and central New Mexico from the morning of the 21st through the day on the 23rd. The first band of snow occurred on the morning of the 21st over far west and northwest sections of the state. Then, a strong cold front swept through the state on the 22nd, bringing a round of heavy snow across much of western and central New Mexico. An additional upper level disturbance crossed the southern portion of the state on the 23rd, bringing central and southern areas some additional snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 4 and 12 inches of snow fell across the Northwest Highlands.  The heaviest snow fell around Cuba.  However, the effects of the cold and snow were felt elsewhere. A 31 year old man died of exposure in Crownpoint on the 23rd. His body was found north of the chapter house with his lower legs and feet touching the snowy ground.

CZ_NAME: NORTHERN SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS ABOVE 9500 FEET/RED RIVER
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third and final storm during the week of January 18th delivered heavy snow to much of western and central New Mexico from the morning of the 21st through the day on the 23rd. The first band of snow occurred on the morning of the 21st over far west and northwest sections of the state. Then, a strong cold front swept through the state on the 22nd, bringing a round of heavy snow across much of western and central New Mexico. An additional upper level disturbance crossed the southern portion of the state on the 23rd, bringing central and southern areas some additional snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ski areas picked up around 18 inches of fresh powder across the northern Sangre de Cristo Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite a respite from winter storms for a few days, icy roads were still a factor across much of northern New Mexico due to several rounds of winter storms the previous week.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 31 year old man was killed after he lost control of his vehicle and it rolled over an icy road near Tesuque. The man was headed south on County Road 73 toward US 84/285 when he slid of the roadway.  He was not wearing a seat belt and was partially ejected through the driver's side window.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful storm off the West Coast moved inland on the 22nd, increasing the pressure gradient in the mid levels of the atmosphere. These winds mixed down to the surface, producing high wind gusts over the south central mountains and eastern plains.

A powerful storm off the West Coast moved inland on the 22nd, increasing the pressure gradient in the mid levels of the atmosphere. These winds mixed down to the surface, producing high wind gusts over the south central mountains and eastern plains.

Strong warm air advection developed across western New Mexico late on the 21st into the early morning hours of the 22nd.  This caused snow to turn to rain, and more than 2 inches of rain fell across the Gila Wilderness.  The heavy rain, combined with some snowmelt, resulted in flooding near the Gila Cliff Dwellings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over 2 inches of rain combined with some snow melt resulted in flooding along State Highway 15.  The approach to the West Fork Bridge was washed out when the West Fork of the Gila River rose out of its banks.  State Highway 15 was closed between the Gila Cliff Dwellings Visitor Center and the trail head for the Gila Cliff Dwellings National Monument.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second storm in two days crossed New Mexico on the 20th, with widespread light to moderate snow. Overall snow amounts were less than the previous storm. Up to eight inches fell over the western and south central Mountains, while up to 6 and 7 inches fell over the northern mountains. Lesser amounts generally fell over the lower elevations of west and central New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to eight inches of snow fell over the Chuska Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing upper level shortwave trough resulted in strong winds in the Guadalupe and Davis Mountains.  Portions of the southeast New Mexico plains were also affected by these stronger winds on 1/22/10 and 1/23/10.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The skirting was ripped off a mobile home in Artesia due to the strong winds.

CZ_NAME: SOUTHERN SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS ABOVE 9500 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first in a series of disturbances crossed northern New Mexico on the evening of the 18th through mid afternoon on the 19th.  An east-west band of lower elevation rain and higher elevation snow developed and slowly moved north across western and central New Mexico overnight. Over eight inches of snow accumulated across the higher terrain, while amounts of 2 to 6 inches were common across the western and northern valleys.  The hardest hit areas stretched from the Jemez Mountains, eastward across the Santa Fe Metro Area, to the southern Sangre De Cristo Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 8 and 14 inches of snow fell across the southern Sangre de Cristo Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second storm in two days crossed New Mexico on the 20th, with widespread light to moderate snow. Overall snow amounts were less than the previous storm. Up to eight inches fell over the western and south central Mountains, while up to 6 and 7 inches fell over the northern mountains. Lesser amounts generally fell over the lower elevations of west and central New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to eight inches of snow fell over the higher peaks of the San Juan Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An impressive storm system moved across northern Baja California on the 27th and crossed New Mexico on the 28th. Plenty of moisture was drawn north into the state, while a strong back door cold front brought chilly air to the eastern plains. This combination resulted in heavy snow from the central mountain chain across the eastern plains to the Texas border. Rain changed to freezing rain and sleet before turning to all snow over portions of Chaves, Roosevelt and Curry counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to 12 inches of snow fell across Union County with drifts to three or four feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An impressive storm system moved across northern Baja California on the 27th and crossed New Mexico on the 28th. Plenty of moisture was drawn north into the state, while a strong back door cold front brought chilly air to the eastern plains. This combination resulted in heavy snow from the central mountain chain across the eastern plains to the Texas border. Rain changed to freezing rain and sleet before turning to all snow over portions of Chaves, Roosevelt and Curry counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 15 inches of snow fell over the eastern portion of San Miguel County with drifts up to three feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong closed upper low pressure system slid south across western New Mexico while dragging a strong back door cold front across the eastern plains.  The northern mountains as well as the east slopes of the central mountain chain were hit the hardest - with some locations receiving up to a foot of snow. The snow was responsible for several road closures, including portions of Interstate 25 and Interstate 40.  State Highway 475, also known as Hyde Park Road, was closed by State Police which stranded travelers returning from a day of skiing at Santa Fe Ski Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximately 1 to 3 inches of snow created slick and hazardous roadways across the West Central Plateau.  A man, age 60, was killed after losing control of his SUV on Interstate 40 near Gallup around 645 am MST.  His wife and grandchildren were also in the vehicle, but survived with only minor injuries.  Almost 30 crashes were reported, though no others resulted in injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid level cutoff low moved slowly over New Mexico and west Texas on 1/22/10 and 1/23/10.  Due to a cold airmass building into the region at the same time, heavy snow fell in portions of the Guadalupe Mountains.  Lesser amounts of sleet and snow fell over the southeast New Mexico plains, the Van Horn area, Davis Mountains, upper Trans Pecos, and Permian Basin of west Texas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mix of rain and sleet changed to snow over the Guadalupe Mountains on 1/23/10.  Queen received 7 inches of snow by 2200 LST on 1/23/10.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over 8 inches of snow was reported across the southern Sangre de Cristo Mountains, though some locations, including the Santa Fe Ski Area, picked up several more inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing upper level shortwave trough resulted in strong winds in the Guadalupe and Davis Mountains.  Portions of the southeast New Mexico plains were also affected by these stronger winds on 1/22/10 and 1/23/10.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained west winds of 40 MPH or greater occurred at Carlsbad from 0953 LST to 1353 LST on 1/23/10.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system blew through New Mexico on April Fool's Day. Nearly all areas east of the central mountain chain recorded wind gusts that reached or exceeded 58 mph.  Blowing dust reduced visibilities to around a mile in many locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A White Sands Missile Range Mesonet site southeast of San Antonio recorded a peak wind gust of 59 mph. The roof of the New Loma Stadium Cinema in Socorro was partially torn off from the high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper level shortwave trough moved over southeast New Mexico and west Texas on February 23rd.  With cold air already entrenched over the area, widespread snowfall occurred.  Snowfall totals ranged from 3 to 7 inches across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of snow was reported in and around Tatum.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With abundant low level moisture in place just east of the dryline, afternoon heating led to the development of scattered thunderstorms over Eddy County.  These storms continued into the evening hours, with some storms reaching severe levels.

A sharp dry line moved west out of west Texas and became stationary over the far eastern plains on Sunday, May 23, 2010.  Meanwhile, an unseasonably deep upper level low pressure system over the Great Basin was moving slowly east toward New Mexico.  The combination of the upper level disturbance approaching New Mexico and extreme instability along the dry line set the stage for widespread severe weather from Quay County northeast into Union County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was damage to agricultural equipment. A single wide mobile home that had tie down straps was completely destroyed and the undercarriage was carried approximately 40 yards. No injuries reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weak satellite tornado captured on video that lasted for approximately 8 minutes. No injuries or significant damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of the 25th, a sharp dryline draped along the east central plains interacted with upper level energy ejecting east out of Arizona.  The combination of strong instability along the dryline and the upper level disturbance produced widespread strong to severe thunderstorms over Curry and Roosevelt Counties.  A few storms continued into the early morning hours of the 26th.

Low level moisture retreated west to the east slopes of the central mountain chain on the 26th.  Moderate instability along the east slopes combined with weak upper level forcing created isolated severe thunderstorms over the northeast highlands during the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was 2 to 3 inches deep along Interstate 25.  Several cars reported off the road.

Low level moisture surged westward to the Continental Divide early on the 28th. An upper level trough moving slowly east out of the Great Basin combined with low level moisture and daytime heating provided sufficient lift to produce very localized severe weather.

A strong low pressure system approached the state late on the 1st, resulting in widespread snow showers across the higher terrain of New Mexico.  Though an inch or 2 was reported across much of the northern and central mountains, western areas were hit the hardest. Snow was heaviest between the early morning and evening hours on the 2nd, where up to 10 inches of snow fell around Fence Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The area around Fence Lake received between 4 and 10 inches of snow, with the greatest snow amounts reported just south of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms were the result of a back door cold front which slid through the eastern plains of New Mexico during the day.  Initially, thunderstorms brought hail and gusty winds across southeast New Mexico.  Then later, the thunderstorms evolved into a cluster which slowly moved east into Texas.  This cluster of storms brought rainfall amounts of up to 2 inches in one hours time across the east.  Later that night, the front pushed through the gaps of the central mountain chain resulting in east winds topping 60 mph.  Tree damage was noted across much of Albuquerque.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several reports of high winds across the Albuquerque Metro Area were reported as the east winds raced through the gaps of the central mountain chain.  In the Albuquerque Foothills, winds were estimated around 70 mph, which blew down a fence, broke numerous large branches, and uprooted at least two trees.  Roof shingle damage also occurred to numerous houses in the foothills. A 60 mph gust was measured just west of Placitas, and a 58 mph wind gust was recorded at the Albuquerque ASOS at 837 pm MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Daytime heating, a mid level moisture axis and orographic lift combined during the afternoon hours to produce thunderstorms across southeastern New Mexico.
Daytime heating, a mid level moisture axis and orographic lift combined during the afternoon hours to produce thunderstorms across southeastern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage was not reported with the wind gust.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of golf balls was reported in Sixteen Springs Canyon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread thunderstorms on the 15th produced some strong wind gusts, resulting in downed trees and tree limbs. Large hail was also reported in Lincoln County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large 4 foot diameter tree was knocked over by the strong wind gusts.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 2-year-old died after being left in a hot car for almost four hours at Southwestern Indian Polytechnic Institute.  By noon MST, the outside air temperature was 93 degrees which may have resulted in temperatures exceeding 135 degrees in the vehicle.

Double Eagle Airport reported a peak wind gust of 62 mph at 1115 am MST and a sustained wind speed of 43 mph from 1115 am to 1215 pm. The Albuquerque Sunport recorded a peak wind gust of 63 mph at 451 pm and a sustained wind speed of 46 mph from 415 pm to 515 pm. Power lines were down near Central and Laguna Place in Albuquerque. A downed power line in southwest Albuquerque started a brush fire and minor damage to a house. Vinyl fabric on the north side of the upper wall of the University of New Mexico's indoor practice facility was ripped off from the winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving upper level storm system and its associated cold front brought very strong winds to the state on the 10th.  Strong winds started early in the morning, with most locations gusting near 60 mph before noon.  The strong winds created areas of blowing dust with reduced visibility down to 2 miles or less at times.  The gusty winds also took down power poles, broke tree limbs and tore a roof off a mobile home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts up to 63 mph were recorded at Sierra Blanca Regional Airport near Ruidoso.

Wind damage occurred in Veguita at 200 pm when several sheets of Pro-Panel roofing were blown off the roof of a trading post along highway 304. A travel trailer was blown over along Interstate 25, 1.5 miles of Socorro at 230 pm MST.

A strong Pacific trough moving into the desert southwest brought a cold front through the region with wind gusts up to 96 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 76 mph was reported at the White Sands Missle Range Post Headquarters. Other reports include a gust to 67 mph at Holloman AFB and a gust to 62 mph reported 5 miles northwest of Orogrande at a Mesonet site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper low moving across the Rockies had a 130 knot jet streak moving around the base of it into southern New Mexico. A strong surface low deepened over the Oklahoma panhandle with winds ahead of a strong cold front gusting up to 95 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak gust of 69 MPH was reported at the Truth or Consequences Airport. In addition, numerous power lines were down across the city.
