EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Easterly winds behind a weak cold front allowed for low level moisture to move into southeast New Mexico. This easterly flow and daytime heating resulted in orographic lift along the Guadalupe Mountains, which produced thunderstorms.

Deep moisture combined with a weak upper level disturbance allowed slow moving thunderstorms to develop across central and western New Mexico.  Torrential rainfall affected many areas, including Gallup, Albuquerque, Los Lunas, and Jemez Springs, where localized flash flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A White Sands Missile Range Mesonet site recorded a 69 mph wind gust at Stallion Army Airfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture combined with a weak upper level disturbance allowed slow moving thunderstorms to develop across central and western New Mexico.  Torrential rainfall affected many areas, including Gallup, Albuquerque, Los Lunas, and Jemez Springs, where localized flash flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Chavez Road and Woodview Drive in Baca as well as Navajo Road in Thoreau were washed out due to heavy rains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along an eastward progressing dryline across west Texas and southeast New Mexico. Although the severe hail threat was only marginal due to high freezing levels, a dry sub-cloud layer resulted in a few severe wind gusts.

Deep moisture combined with a weak upper level disturbance allowed slow moving thunderstorms to develop across central and western New Mexico.  Torrential rainfall affected many areas, including Gallup, Albuquerque, Los Lunas, and Jemez Springs, where localized flash flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sweetwater, Iyanbito, and Dakota Loop Roads in Iyanbito were washed out due to flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoon moisture combined with an easterly wave over southeast New Mexico to create widespread heavy rain across northern and central New Mexico. Hardest hit areas were in the vicinity of the central mountain chain, where several flash floods were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large rocks were swept onto State Route 48, one mile south of Alto, by rushing water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low level moisture in place across the mountainous terrain of west Texas and southeast New Mexico combined with very warm afternoon temperatures to produce sufficient instability for thunderstorm development. A few of these storms were severe, resulting in damaging winds and hail.
A back door cold front delivered even more moisture to eastern New Mexico, resulting in thunderstorms producing heavy rain and high wind gusts, along with penny size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranged from peas to nickels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture in place across southeast New Mexico combined with weak upper level disturbances and surface boundaries to produce scattered thunderstorms during the early morning hours.  Due to the slow movement of the thunderstorms and unseasonably high atmospheric moisture content, flash flooding accompanied some of the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported 2 feet of water over the intersection of SH 137 and US 285, about 18 miles west northwest of Carlsbad, NM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Plenty of moisture remained across eastern New Mexico behind the previous days back door cold front. Thunderstorms with heavy rain pounded western San Miguel County, resulting in a flash flood in Bernal, knocking out bridges on county roads B27 and B28.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains caused the Pecos River to overflow its banks from Ribera south to nearly Villanueva. One home in Ribera was moderately damaged and two others had minor damage. Route 3, which runs along the Pecos River, was mostly under water.  Three other roads were washed out in Ribera. Areas along and upstream from the Pecos received heavy rain, but so did many creeks and tributaries that flow into the river.  Roads along these secondary creeks sustained as much, if not more, damage than in the town of Ribera.  In Bernal, flooding creeks caused damage to County Roads B27 and B28A.  Only one lane remained open after the shoulders were washed away.  The flooding also washed out 300 feet of railway along the Burlington Northern Santa Fe Railroad near Bernal.  Further upstream, flash flooding along Tecolote Creek caused one bridge to collapse and damaged five low water crossings. The fast moving water was 12 feet outside of its banks.  Another low water crossing in Ojito Fritos along County Road A19A had standing water because culverts were blocked by debris.  Despite the mud and debris along the road, one lane was kept open.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High based showers over Albuquerque produced plenty of virga during the early evening, resulting in strong and erratic wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The dry microburst winds severely damaged a large tank that was under construction on the Kirtland Air Force Base, which is at the eastern end of the Albuquerque Sunport. The tank was 85 feet in diameter and 48 feet tall, but did not have a roof, yet. Three trees were also uprooted at the VA hospital on Gibson Street.  Near this location, a portion of a fence was blown over onto neighboring vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary frontal boundary was located across the northern Permian Basin, and a dryline was present across far west Texas into southeast New Mexico.  These features, in combination with a shortwave trough moving through the area, provided a focus for thunderstorm development. With an unstable atmosphere in place across the area, thunderstorms produced several reports of severe hail.

Low level moisture in place across the mountainous terrain of west Texas and southeast New Mexico combined with very warm afternoon temperatures to produce sufficient instability for thunderstorm development. A few of these storms were severe, resulting in damaging winds and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A window was blown out at the Brantley Lake State Park office building.  The winds were estimated to be near 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low level moisture in place across the mountainous terrain of west Texas and southeast New Mexico combined with very warm afternoon temperatures to produce sufficient instability for thunderstorm development. A few of these storms were severe, resulting in damaging winds and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A horse trailer was blown off the road, in addition to a power pole being snapped and a yield sign being damaged at the intersection of SH 137 and US 285.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough provided a focus for thunderstorm development across southeast New Mexico.  The combination of unstable conditions and a dry sub-cloud layer from daytime heating resulted in a high-end severe thunderstorm wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small barn was blow over about 5 miles southeast of Artesia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low level moisture in place across the mountainous terrain of west Texas and southeast New Mexico combined with very warm afternoon temperatures to produce sufficient instability for thunderstorm development. A few of these storms were severe, resulting in damaging winds and hail.

Low level moisture in place across the mountainous terrain of west Texas and southeast New Mexico combined with very warm afternoon temperatures to produce sufficient instability for thunderstorm development. A few of these storms were severe, resulting in damaging winds and hail.

Low level moisture in place across the mountainous terrain of west Texas and southeast New Mexico combined with very warm afternoon temperatures to produce sufficient instability for thunderstorm development. A few of these storms were severe, resulting in damaging winds and hail.

Low level moisture in place across the mountainous terrain of west Texas and southeast New Mexico combined with very warm afternoon temperatures to produce sufficient instability for thunderstorm development. A few of these storms were severe, resulting in damaging winds and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several homes, vehicles and tractors had their windows broken by the hail.  In addition, damage was also done to roofs and siding on homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low level moisture in place across the mountainous terrain of west Texas and southeast New Mexico combined with very warm afternoon temperatures to produce sufficient instability for thunderstorm development. A few of these storms were severe, resulting in damaging winds and hail.

Low level moisture in place across the mountainous terrain of west Texas and southeast New Mexico combined with very warm afternoon temperatures to produce sufficient instability for thunderstorm development. A few of these storms were severe, resulting in damaging winds and hail.

Moist southeasterly low level flow coupled with an approaching upper level trough allowed numerous showers and thunderstorms to develop across eastern New Mexico.  Storms began early in the morning, where hail and even a tornado was reported across the southeast. Storms continued into the afternoon, where up to golf ball size hail was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several transformers and power lines were damaged from the high wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture and some low level convergence helped spark thunderstorms over the eastern plains. A few of these storms turned severe with damaging wind gusts and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few portable buildings near Logan were overturned.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture combined with a weak upper level disturbance allowed slow moving thunderstorms to develop across central and western New Mexico.  Torrential rainfall affected many areas, including Gallup, Albuquerque, Los Lunas, and Jemez Springs, where localized flash flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to eight inches of flowing water and mud impacted Main Street near Interstate 25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture combined with a weak upper level disturbance allowed slow moving thunderstorms to develop across central and western New Mexico.  Torrential rainfall affected many areas, including Gallup, Albuquerque, Los Lunas, and Jemez Springs, where localized flash flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A drainage ditch along N. Los Lentes Road backed up into one house, meanwhile water was overflowing a brick fence into a residents yard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture and some low level convergence helped spark thunderstorms over the eastern plains. A few of these storms turned severe with damaging wind gusts and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree, 2.5-3 feet in diameter, was blown down near Broadview.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread thunderstorms on the 15th produced some strong wind gusts, resulting in downed trees and tree limbs. Large hail was also reported in Lincoln County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several tree branches larger than 2 inches in diameter were broken off of trees from wind gusts estimated around 60 mph. In addition, high winds knocked the roof off of an abandoned single wide trailer, which fell onto the roof a neighboring house on Blue Canyon Dr.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of the 25th, a sharp dryline draped along the east central plains interacted with upper level energy ejecting east out of Arizona.  The combination of strong instability along the dryline and the upper level disturbance produced widespread strong to severe thunderstorms over Curry and Roosevelt Counties.  A few storms continued into the early morning hours of the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report relayed by Emergency Manager. Tornado briefly touched down approximately 0.25 mi west of NM/TX state line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Easterly winds behind a weak cold front allowed for low level moisture to move into southeast New Mexico. This easterly flow and daytime heating resulted in orographic lift along the Guadalupe Mountains, which produced thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several side roll irrigation sprinklers were destroyed by a strong wind gust in Atoka, NM.  The time of this report was estimated according to radar and another wind gust recorded by the Artesia ASOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A back door cold front delivered even more moisture to eastern New Mexico, resulting in thunderstorms producing heavy rain and high wind gusts, along with penny size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was 18 inches of standing water on 18th St. in the southern portion of Portales.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of the 25th, a sharp dryline draped along the east central plains interacted with upper level energy ejecting east out of Arizona.  The combination of strong instability along the dryline and the upper level disturbance produced widespread strong to severe thunderstorms over Curry and Roosevelt Counties.  A few storms continued into the early morning hours of the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Relayed by Emergency Manager. Tornado briefly touched down in open rangeland. No damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper trough approaching from the west provided enough instability for strong thunderstorms to develop over the Gila region of New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three-quarter inch hail was reported 1 mile east of Lake Roberts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sharp dry line moved west out of west Texas and became stationary over the far eastern plains on Sunday, May 23, 2010.  Meanwhile, an unseasonably deep upper level low pressure system over the Great Basin was moving slowly east toward New Mexico.  The combination of the upper level disturbance approaching New Mexico and extreme instability along the dry line set the stage for widespread severe weather from Quay County northeast into Union County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Debris on Highway 56 was seen at mile post 93. Power lines were down, barbed wire fences and hay bales were all over.  In addition, a steel building from a feed lot was destroyed, a cow with a broken leg was observed on the road, and a semi truck was turned over on private property. The tornado crossed the New Mexico-Oklahoma state line approximately 8.7 miles southeast of Seneca and continued into neighboring Cimarron County, Oklahoma.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture combined with an upper level wave sliding north across western New Mexico produced more torrential rainfall and repeat flooding for areas around Gallup and the Jemez Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding resulted in mud and rocks, some as large as 10 inches in diameter, flowing across portions of Highway 4 between Jemez Pueblo and Jemez Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled over the eastern plains, along with an approaching trough, brought moisture, lift and instability to far eastern New Mexico on the 12th. Numerous large hail was observed along with a few high wind reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hailstorm left much of the Clovis area battered by hail up to the size of golf balls.  The majority of the damage occurred in the northeastern portion of the community.  Over 1600 home and auto claims were submitted to insurance companies.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled over the eastern plains, along with an approaching trough, brought moisture, lift and instability to far eastern New Mexico on the 12th. Numerous large hail was observed along with a few high wind reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cars were stranded on Main Street and Highway 209 in Clovis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level trough crossing the central Rockies combined with deep low level moisture across the Eastern Plains produced isolated severe thunderstorms late Wednesday afternoon.
A weak upper level trough crossing the central Rockies combined with deep low level moisture across the Eastern Plains produced isolated severe thunderstorms late Wednesday afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple large tree branches were knocked down. A 12 inch diameter cottonwood tree was reduced to less than half its original height.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level trough crossing the central Rockies combined with deep low level moisture across the Eastern Plains produced isolated severe thunderstorms late Wednesday afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Frost Ranch SchoolNet observation recorded a 62 mph wind gusts on a 34 foot tower.  Observation was relayed by KVII in Amarillo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms were the result of a back door cold front which slid through the eastern plains of New Mexico during the day.  Initially, thunderstorms brought hail and gusty winds across southeast New Mexico.  Then later, the thunderstorms evolved into a cluster which slowly moved east into Texas.  This cluster of storms brought rainfall amounts of up to 2 inches in one hours time across the east.  Later that night, the front pushed through the gaps of the central mountain chain resulting in east winds topping 60 mph.  Tree damage was noted across much of Albuquerque.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 2-acre fire resulted in damage of the Cumbres and Toltec Scenic Railroad by the Lobato Trestle.  The fire, which was approximately 5 miles north-northeast of Chama near the Colorado border, destroyed the wooden ties that support the rail bed.  As a result, the railroad had to halt train operations through the area. The cause of the fire remains unknown, though arson and natural causes have been ruled out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoon moisture produced thunderstorms with heavy rain over northwest New Mexico. This resulted in flash flooding across State Road 511 northwest of Navajo Dam.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was running water, rocks and a mudslide over State Road 511 between mile markers 3 and 7. One vehicle hit the rocks which ruptured its fuel tank.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense thunderstorm complex that developed southeast of the Farmington area shortly before 7 pm MST moved northwest into the Four Corners region through 9 pm MST.  Significant flash flooding was reported across Farmington and nearby areas as rainfall rates exceeded 2 inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding damaged Manuelito Canyon Road and nearby Manuelito Canyon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense thunderstorm complex that developed southeast of the Farmington area shortly before 7 pm MST moved northwest into the Four Corners region through 9 pm MST.  Significant flash flooding was reported across Farmington and nearby areas as rainfall rates exceeded 2 inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flooding plagued the Farmington area during the evening hours of August 1st.  More than 2.5 inches of rain fell in 3 hours, but most of the rain fell in about a 30 minute time frame.  Some of the hardest hit areas are described in the following paragraphs. ||The Webb Chevrolet-Toyota dealership and neighboring businesses along East Main Street between Gila St. and Hannon Dr. was described as a swamp.  Weeds and shrubs were wrapped around the tires of each truck as water, mud and debris flooded into the lot.  The force of the water moved one truck about six feet, and moved a nearby dumpster approximately 100 yards.  Also in this area, a man whose car got stuck at the intersection of Cliffside Dr. and East Main got trapped in shallow, quick moving water. The man was rescued by firefighters and was unharmed.||Near the intersection of Largo St. and Railroad St., water was estimated to be 6 to 12 inches deep at the height of the event.  Water damage was evident to at least two residences. ||An arroyo that crosses Hubbard St. near Peace Valley Road was flowing over the roadway. A high school senior attempted to drive through the waters, when her car was washed away.  Her car traveled approximately 80 yards down the arroyo before she was rescued.  She was unharmed, but her car was destroyed.  After the waters receded, it became evident that Peace Valley Road was partially eroded away beneath the pavement.||Due to a holding pond that overflowed, five homes around the Country Club suffered significant damage, including one basement that was completely filled with water. Most of these homes were filled with no less than 6 inches of water on the ground level, resulting in damage of carpet, tile and other objects or furniture that was on the floor. Landscaping was completely eroded away, and fence lines were downed.||A mudslide was also reported on County Road 3000 at mile marker 9.5.  The road was impassible until the mud was cleaned off the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense thunderstorm complex that developed southeast of the Farmington area shortly before 7 pm MST moved northwest into the Four Corners region through 9 pm MST.  Significant flash flooding was reported across Farmington and nearby areas as rainfall rates exceeded 2 inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lori Drive just northeast of Bluewater Lake was washed out due to heavy rains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense thunderstorm complex that developed southeast of the Farmington area shortly before 7 pm MST moved northwest into the Four Corners region through 9 pm MST.  Significant flash flooding was reported across Farmington and nearby areas as rainfall rates exceeded 2 inches per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An arroyo washed over the roadway at intersection of Highway 550 and County Road 7150.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoon moisture combined with an easterly wave over southeast New Mexico to create widespread heavy rain across northern and central New Mexico. Hardest hit areas were in the vicinity of the central mountain chain, where several flash floods were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 16 year old boy was swept down the Embudo Arroyo by rapid flowing flood waters from near Juan Tabo and Candelaria to Carlisle and Interstate 40. This was a distance of five miles and the water was estimated to be flowing at 35 mph. The boy only received a few cuts and bruises.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level trough moved through southern New Mexico in a moderately unstable environment to produce a severe thunderstorm over the Gila which produced a microburst with estimated winds of 80 to 90 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A newly placed trailer was overturned and blown off supports and a large trampoline was removed from property on south side of Gila. Another mobile home near River Road suffered significant roof damage and a tree was uprooted on the property. Numerous flattened yucca stalks were reported. ||An uprooted pine tree was observed on a property along highway 153 between Hooker Loop and Lee Road. Hail up to a half inch in diameter was also observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very efficient rain-producing thunderstorms slowly moved eastward from near Santa Fe across the southern Sangre de Cristo Mountains over the Tecolote Fire burn scar. Over two inches of rain fell across a relatively large area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains carried logs across a private roadway about one half mile downstream of the Tecolote Fire burn area, or about 1.5 miles north of Blue Haven Church Camp.  The road was impassable for a time due to the logs and high water.  The heavy rains also washed debris from the burn scar into Blue Creek, which doubled in size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep layer of moisture over northeast New Mexico helped to fuel thunderstorms with heavy rain over Union and Santa Fe counties. This resulted in the flooding of State Highway 370 from Clayton to Clayton Lake State Park, as well as caused the Santa Fe River to swell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Route 370 was impassable due to water covering much of the road between Clayton and Clayton Lake State Park, as several creeks overflowed their banks. Several motorists were stranded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level disturbance moved through southern New Mexico in a modestly moist and unstable environment. A weak surface trough was also present which helped aid in thunderstorm development over the lower elevations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hangar at the Columbus Airport was damaged due to wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep layer of moisture over northeast New Mexico helped to fuel thunderstorms with heavy rain over Union and Santa Fe counties. This resulted in the flooding of State Highway 370 from Clayton to Clayton Lake State Park, as well as caused the Santa Fe River to swell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple driving home from dinner was caught in the swollen Santa Fe River at San Ysidro Crossing.  The couple attempted to drive through the flooded roadway, when the river surged and a tree came down changing the flow of the river.  Santa Fe fire department successfully rescued the couple who only sustained minor injuries.  At the time of the rescue, the water was up to the car windows.  After the rescue, the car floated and flipped over San Ysidro Falls, where later it was found to be totaled.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist environment was in place under an upper level ridge. A stationary boundary was draped across southern New Mexico providing the lift for slow moving thunderstorms which caused numerous reports of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous buildings and streets were flooded through the town of Truth or Consequences.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist environment was in place under an upper level ridge. A stationary boundary was draped across southern New Mexico providing the lift for slow moving thunderstorms which caused numerous reports of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain over Las Cruces flooded many buildings and streets across town. New Mexico State University recorded 3.36 inches of rain. At least 6 buildings on the campus had water damage with several roads closed. Several water rescues from stalled vehicles were performed on campus.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist environment was in place on west side of upper ridge with a weak disturbance moving across southern New Mexico allowing for heavy rain producing thunderstorms to develop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water up to 2 inches deep surrounding a house after 0.74 inches of rain fell in 10 minutes 7 miles northwest of Silver City. Also, 1.5 inches of rain fell in 30 minutes 6 miles north of Silver City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms over eastern New Mexico produced heavy rain and a few wind gusts, mainly around Cannon Air Force Base.
A lone severe storm produced copious amounts of rain and large hail at Sugarite Canyon State Park near the Colorado border.

A deep subtropical moisture tap combined with weak steering flow allowed widespread heavy rainfall to develop across much of northern and central New Mexico.  Training storms over the southeast corner of the state created torrential rainfall and localized flash flooding. Flash Flooding was also observed along the higher terrain of the central and west central mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large area of one foot deep water along Highway 267 between mile markers 6 and 8 made the road impassible.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep subtropical moisture tap combined with weak steering flow allowed widespread heavy rainfall to develop across much of northern and central New Mexico.  Training storms over the southeast corner of the state created torrential rainfall and localized flash flooding. Flash Flooding was also observed along the higher terrain of the central and west central mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Arroyo del Oso Golf Course at Osuna and Louisiana flooded. Lots of debris on course from Bear Canyon Arroyo. Ducks were actually swimming in standing water at hole number seven.  Intersection of San Francisco and Barstow also flooded with traffic stopped due to flowing water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued above normal moisture coupled with slow moving thunderstorms resulted in a few flash floods across western New Mexico during the afternoon and evening hours.  Though many of the storms spanned a relatively small area, they dumped upwards of 2 inches of rain in some spots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain eroded the smaller of two uranium tailing piles at the Homestead/Barrick Gold former mill site. A wall of water 80 to 100 yards wide flooded the four subdivisions. Ralph Card, Sundowner, Thunderbird and Malapais roads were flooded and several other roads in the area had high water.  Residents of the four subdivisions likened their front and back yards to lakes with water up to 18 inches deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued above normal moisture coupled with slow moving thunderstorms resulted in a few flash floods across western New Mexico during the afternoon and evening hours.  Though many of the storms spanned a relatively small area, they dumped upwards of 2 inches of rain in some spots.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cox Canyon Bridge on County Road 2900 just southwest of Cedar Hill collapsed.  Officials say the bridge collapsed because water running down the canyon hit the bridge at an angle and the eddy it created, ripped out the structure's support.  The residents of about 100 homes were left stranded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very efficient rain-producing thunderstorms slowly moved eastward from near Santa Fe across the southern Sangre de Cristo Mountains over the Tecolote Fire burn scar. Over two inches of rain fell across a relatively large area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A garage on Avalon Place flooded due to heavy rain.  Fortunately, flooding to the the interior of the house was avoided because mechanical pumps were brought in to mitigate the disaster.  Just north of this area, 2.32 inches of rain was reported in the Santa Fe Seton Village.  This rain fell in just over one hours time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist easterly upslope flow with an upper level ridge centered over area allowed for slow moving thunderstorms to produce very heavy rains over the Sacramento Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm drainage culverts on Highway 130 near mile marker 16 were plugged and 2 feet of water was flowing over the highway. The highway was impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface trough extended from Northwestern Mexico through Southwest New Mexico and provided enough lift for thunderstorms over Hidalgo County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to nickel sized hail covered the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: South-southwesterly flow ahead of a weak low over Baja brought deep moisture and lift into the region. The thunderstorms that developed were slow moving and produced very heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Silver City Sun-News reported heavy rains caused significant damage to a section of Forest Road 150 at Rocky Canyon in the Gila National Forest. The Rocky Canyon Campground was inaccessible due to downed logs, debris and large ruts blocking the campground entrance.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level jet stream translated east across the central Rockies early on the 25th.  An intense surface pressure gradient developed across New Mexico as a deep surface low pressure center developed over western Kansas.  Widespread high winds were reported across the area with peak wind gusts over 70 mph reported along the East Slopes.

A powerful upper level jet stream translated east across the central Rockies early on the 25th.  An intense surface pressure gradient developed across New Mexico as a deep surface low pressure center developed over western Kansas.  Widespread high winds were reported across the area with peak wind gusts over 70 mph reported along the East Slopes.

An intense surface pressure gradient developed across New Mexico as a deep surface low pressure center developed over western Kansas.  Widespread high winds were reported across the area with peak wind gusts over 70 mph reported along the East Slopes.

An intense surface pressure gradient developed across New Mexico as a deep surface low pressure center developed over western Kansas.  Widespread high winds were reported across the area with peak wind gusts over 70 mph reported along the East Slopes.

An intense surface pressure gradient developed across New Mexico as a deep surface low pressure center developed over western Kansas.  Widespread high winds were reported across the area with peak wind gusts over 70 mph reported along the East Slopes.

A powerful upper level jet stream translated east across the central Rockies early on the 25th.  An intense surface pressure gradient developed across New Mexico as a deep surface low pressure center developed over western Kansas.  Widespread high winds were reported across the area with peak wind gusts over 70 mph reported along the East Slopes.

A strong cutoff low was located over far Southwest Arizona. The nose of a 100 knot jet was rounding the low and moving into Southern New Mexico and Far West Texas. A surface trough was also located near the Rio Grande Valley.  All of these features combined to produce severe thunderstorms with hail to the size of baseballs reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet site located 18 miles north-northeast of White Sands Main Post reported wind gust of 66 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level jet stream translated east across the central Rockies early on the 25th.  An intense surface pressure gradient developed across New Mexico as a deep surface low pressure center developed over western Kansas.  Widespread high winds were reported across the area with peak wind gusts over 70 mph reported along the East Slopes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs broke and large aspen trees fell and snapped power lines in and around Los Alamos as winds gusted to 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorms producing very heavy rainfall developed during the late afternoon hours across the Navajo Nation near the Arizona state line. The heavy rains led to flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Navajo Route 134 was impassible due to high water at mile marker 22.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper level trough crossed the northern Rocky Mountains, moisture surged northward across New Mexico.  Initially, a severe storm with hail and very heavy rain formed over the Sacramento Mountains.  Then, while the associated cold front moved down the eastern plains, a few prolific lightning producing storms impacted the Rio Grande Valley with heavy rain and hail.

A strong cutoff low was located over far Southwest Arizona. The nose of a 100 knot jet was rounding the low and moving into Southern New Mexico and Far West Texas. A surface trough was also located near the Rio Grande Valley.  All of these features combined to produce severe thunderstorms with hail to the size of baseballs reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 58 mph wind gust was reported 10 miles east-northeast of the White Sands Main Post at a SAMS Mesonet site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong to severe thunderstorms marched eastward across northern San Juan County during the afternoon of the 8th.  Though the storms were moving east-northeast around 20 mph, they managed to drop significant amounts of rainfall in a short amount of time.  Flash floods were reported north of Farmington, and east of Bloomfield.  Winds also overturned a mobile home near Shiprock.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mobile home was overturned just south of U.S. 64 in Shiprock. The trailer had just moved to this location one month prior, and was not yet anchored down nor hooked up to any utilities. It sat atop it's wheels on one end and concrete blocks on another.  There was no mobile home skirt around the trailer to prevent wind from going underneath.  It appeared that the trailer rolled off the concrete blocks onto its side, and then again onto its roof. Two people were inside, but escaped without any major injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper level trough crossed the northern Rocky Mountains, moisture surged northward across New Mexico.  Initially, a severe storm with hail and very heavy rain formed over the Sacramento Mountains.  Then, while the associated cold front moved down the eastern plains, a few prolific lightning producing storms impacted the Rio Grande Valley with heavy rain and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Road 1 was impassible as water was quickly running over the road and lapping up along the Tse Bonito bridge.  Though the bridge remains standing, the water tore off both sides of the bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level jet stream translated east across the central Rockies early on the 25th.  An intense surface pressure gradient developed across New Mexico as a deep surface low pressure center developed over western Kansas.  Widespread high winds were reported across the area with peak wind gusts over 70 mph reported along the East Slopes.

As an upper level trough crossed the northern Rocky Mountains, moisture surged northward across New Mexico.  Initially, a severe storm with hail and very heavy rain formed over the Sacramento Mountains.  Then, while the associated cold front moved down the eastern plains, a few prolific lightning producing storms impacted the Rio Grande Valley with heavy rain and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearly 2 inches of rain fell across portions of northwest Rio Rancho.  The intersection of Northern and Idalia was closed due to flash flooding.  Along Unser, though the road was not closed, it was impassible to several cars which got stuck in the high waters.  A sewer line was also damaged, which allowed sewage to flow into the Montoya's Arroyo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong to severe thunderstorms marched eastward across northern San Juan County during the afternoon of the 8th.  Though the storms were moving east-northeast around 20 mph, they managed to drop significant amounts of rainfall in a short amount of time.  Flash floods were reported north of Farmington, and east of Bloomfield.  Winds also overturned a mobile home near Shiprock.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred along CR4990. A damage survey found that several arroyos flow from the higher terrain to the south of the road northward into the San Juan River. Though radar indicates that the brunt of the precipitation fell to the south of the road, the road became impassible at several locations as there was evidence that a large swath of mud and water flowed across the road, underneath some nearby raised residences.  Since these homes were elevated, they did not receive any damage inside. The storm moved east-northeast near the intersection of CR4990 and CR4550, just south of Blanco, where there was some evidence that high waters flowed there recently.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong to severe thunderstorms marched eastward across northern San Juan County during the afternoon of the 8th.  Though the storms were moving east-northeast around 20 mph, they managed to drop significant amounts of rainfall in a short amount of time.  Flash floods were reported north of Farmington, and east of Bloomfield.  Winds also overturned a mobile home near Shiprock.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One home was flooded approximately 9 miles north of Farmington on Highway 170. Heavy rainfall, combined with insufficient drainage along the highway, resulted in water flowing across the roadway and into the house. High water marks suggest the water was around 10 inches deep, which destroyed carpet and other objects on the floor.  Water continued to flow eastward along a one lane dirt road into a 5 acre, 12 foot deep pond, which was empty prior to the storm. The runoff filled the pond entirely.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread thunderstorms generally moved from west to east across the Land of Enchantment during the afternoon and evening hours.  The stronger storms produced hail up to the size of nickels, heavy rain and wind gusts up to 50 mph. Curb deep water was reported across many areas across the state, though more substantial flooding was noted around Acoma.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several dirt and gravel roads were washed out completely along the northern periphery of the Acoma Indian Reservation and points just north of Acomita.  Much of the damage occurred where arroyos crossed or neared the roadway. The greatest damage occurred  along Indian Service Roads 34, 345, 30 and 26 as well as Stock Yard Road. Water continued to spill over what was left of the roadways days later.  Minor damage was also noted on the Wastewater Treatment Facilities property.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level jet stream translated east across the central Rockies early on the 25th.  An intense surface pressure gradient developed across New Mexico as a deep surface low pressure center developed over western Kansas.  Widespread high winds were reported across the area with peak wind gusts over 70 mph reported along the East Slopes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is the Sandia Peak Tram observation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level jet stream translated east across the central Rockies early on the 25th.  An intense surface pressure gradient developed across New Mexico as a deep surface low pressure center developed over western Kansas.  Widespread high winds were reported across the area with peak wind gusts over 70 mph reported along the East Slopes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observed wind gusts were recorded between 61 and 64 mph for nearly 2 hours.  This is the strongest gust reported. Damage to an antenna was reported on the spotters property.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observation was taken from a Weathernet site at the Vaughn Junior Senior High School.

A powerful upper level jet stream translated east across the central Rockies early on the 25th.  An intense surface pressure gradient developed across New Mexico as a deep surface low pressure center developed over western Kansas. A sharp trough of low pressure from the west combined with remnant moisture from tropical storm Georgette produced heavy rain over much of New Mexico.  Areas around Gladstone in Union County were the hardest hit. Three feet of water inundated one house and rapidly flowing water carried a car 1/4 mile away from the residence.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 27-year old man was swept away by fast flowing water in the I-40 Channel.  The man was walking in the arroyo with his brother near I-40 and Louisiana, when a rush of water swept the man off his feet.  The man traveled west to the North Diversion Channel, then north to the Rio Grande River.  He was found dead along a sand bar in the Rio Grande River three days later near Coors Bypass. He traveled a total distance of 13 miles.  His brother made it out alive, without injury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture combined with an upper level wave sliding north across western New Mexico produced more torrential rainfall and repeat flooding for areas around Gallup and the Jemez Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The La Cueva fire station flooded and Twisted Juniper Road was washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level jet stream translated east across the central Rockies early on the 25th.  An intense surface pressure gradient developed across New Mexico as a deep surface low pressure center developed over western Kansas.  Widespread high winds were reported across the area with peak wind gusts over 70 mph reported along the East Slopes.
East Canyon winds resulted in wind gusts exceeding 58 mph at the mouth of Embudo Canyon for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the east canyon wind came through the gaps of the central mountain chain during the early morning hours, a Davis weather station recorded a 64 mph wind gust at the mouth of Embudo Canyon at an elevation of 5925 feet.  Other gusts exceeding 58 mph were recorded for nearly a 3 hour period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sharp trough of low pressure from the west combined with remnant moisture from tropical storm Georgette produced heavy rain over much of New Mexico.  Areas around Gladstone in Union County were the hardest hit. Three feet of water inundated one house and rapidly flowing water carried a car 1/4 mile away from the residence.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were washed out around Gladstone. A house 8 miles south southeast of Gladstone had three feet of water in it and the owner had his Mercedes Benz car carried one quarter mile away by rapidly flowing water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow-moving and heavy rain-producing thunderstorms developed across northern Chaves and Roosevelt County during the early morning hours.  The heaviest rain fell east of Rogers and north of Causey.  Six miles east of Rogers, 3.40 inches of rain fell in just under 3 hours, causing flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads were washed out 6 miles east of Rogers as 3.40 inches of rain fell in 2 hours and 50 minutes.  2.75 inches of rain was also reported 3 miles north-northeast of Causey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnants of Tropical Depression Georgette moved inland from the Gulf of California and near west Texas providing moisture across the region, as a mid-level shortwave trough moved through southeast New Mexico and west Texas.  The high moisture content contributed to torrential rains from showers and thunderstorms. This activity moved across the same locations resulting in flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain occured across Eddy county with 2.5 to 3 feet of flowing water across Dark canyon road and Hidalgo road.  This contributed to a high water rescue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnants of Tropical Depression Georgette moved inland from the Gulf of California and near west Texas providing moisture across the region, as a mid-level shortwave trough moved through southeast New Mexico and west Texas.  The high moisture content contributed to torrential rains from showers and thunderstorms. This activity moved across the same locations resulting in flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 4:10 PM MST, a trained weather spotter relayed a report of up to 1 1/2' of water on Mesa Street in Carlsbad.  A car was also stalled/flooded at the intersection of Blodgett and Mesquite Street.  Flooding continued almost through 9:00 PM MST across Carlsbad.  One foot of flowing water was reported to be flowing over Blodgett and Pate Streets at 8:40 PM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful upper level jet stream translated east across the central Rockies early on the 25th.  An intense surface pressure gradient developed across New Mexico as a deep surface low pressure center developed over western Kansas.  Widespread high winds were reported across the area with peak wind gusts over 70 mph reported along the East Slopes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The high wind gusts caused numerous trees and tree limbs to break and knock down power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cutoff low was located over far Southwest Arizona. The nose of a 100 knot jet was rounding the low and moving into Southern New Mexico and Far West Texas. A surface trough was also located near the Rio Grande Valley.  All of these features combined to produce severe thunderstorms with hail to the size of baseballs reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Border Patrol reported ping pong ball sized hail at the checkpoint on Highway US54. Hail covered the ground and impeded traffic for some time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cutoff low was located over far Southwest Arizona. The nose of a 100 knot jet was rounding the low and moving into Southern New Mexico and Far West Texas. A surface trough was also located near the Rio Grande Valley.  All of these features combined to produce severe thunderstorms with hail to the size of baseballs reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bureau of Land Management employee reported baseball sized hail on highway 506 about 1 mile east of Highway US54. Vehicle was damaged due to the hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The same upper low pressure system that resulted in hail across western New Mexico on the 20th, neared and moved over the state on the 21st.  Two storms turned severe with better moisture and shear to work with. One storm, over the northeast heights of Albuquerque, dropped up to quarter size hail.  Then later, a lone, slow moving storm over central Chaves County, produced two tornadoes.  Fortunately, these tornadoes occurred over rural areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SKYWARN spotters and several members of the general public filmed the tornado while looking north on Highway 246.  The tornado was also viewable from the town of Roswell.  This tornado primarily impacted the Salt Creek Basin for approximately nine minutes.  This EF-0 tornado had peak winds around 65 mph. No significant damage or injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: SKYWARN spotters and emergency management officials noted a second tornado while looking north from Highway 246.  This tornado began just west of Highway 285 and north of Verbena Road. Peak winds were estimated around 65 mph. No significant damage or injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ground was about 50 percent covered in large hail.  The hail broke a skylight and damaged roof shingles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lone severe thunderstorm developed near San Felipe Pueblo and moved east-southeast along the east mountains.  Hail up to 2 inches in diameter fell and devastated trees, roofs, windshields and windows across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs greater than 2 inches in diameter were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lone severe thunderstorm developed near San Felipe Pueblo and moved east-southeast along the east mountains.  Hail up to 2 inches in diameter fell and devastated trees, roofs, windshields and windows across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kewa Pueblo, formerly known as Santa Domingo Pueblo, received golf ball sized hail which accumulated six to eight inches deep. This caused several roofs on the Pueblo to cave in and weaken some walls. The combination of heavy rain and accumulated hail resulted in some roads being washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture and instability ahead of a potent short wave trough produced large hail around the Four Corners region.

A lone severe thunderstorm developed near San Felipe Pueblo and moved east-southeast along the east mountains.  Hail up to 2 inches in diameter fell and devastated trees, roofs, windshields and windows across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail accumulated 2 inches deep on the ground. Over 200 houses sustained significant damage including roof and window damage. Multiple vehicles were also dented and damaged by the large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another back door front dropped south across the eastern plains, setting up a boundary for thunderstorms to form. A few of the storms turned severe in Curry and Quay counties.
Deep moisture and some low level convergence helped spark thunderstorms over the eastern plains. A few of these storms turned severe with damaging wind gusts and large hail.
Another back door front dropped south across the eastern plains, setting up a boundary for thunderstorms to form. A few of the storms turned severe in Curry and Quay counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind knocked down a large tree and destroyed a shed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist environment was in place on west side of upper ridge with a weak disturbance moving across southern New Mexico allowing for heavy rain producing thunderstorms to develop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water overflowing onto Highway 11 from adjacent drainage ditches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread thunderstorms on the 15th produced some strong wind gusts, resulting in downed trees and tree limbs. Large hail was also reported in Lincoln County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was reported along U.S. Highway 380.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture over eastern New Mexico fueled thunderstorm development on the 18th, with some of the storms producing heavy rain. This resulted in flash flooding east of Encino on Highway 285/60.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water up to one foot deep covered Highway 285/60 between Encino and Vaughn from 530 pm to 600 pm MST.  Spotters reported large debris along the roadway, and significant fence line damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoon moisture combined with an easterly wave over southeast New Mexico to create widespread heavy rain across northern and central New Mexico. Hardest hit areas were in the vicinity of the central mountain chain, where several flash floods were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to 12 inches of water was on several roads northeast of Alto in the Rancho Ruidoso subdivision. Some of the worst hit roadways include County Road D003, Little Creek Road, and Little Creek Hills Road.  At least one house and one barn flooded, and several other homes in the area had minor damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture combined with a weak upper level disturbance allowed slow moving thunderstorms to develop across central and western New Mexico.  Torrential rainfall affected many areas, including Gallup, Albuquerque, Los Lunas, and Jemez Springs, where localized flash flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four trees washed into Kutz Canyon along with portion of a neighboring horse corral. Four feet of the embankment was eroded away.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Depression #2 passed over southeast New Mexico during the overnight hours, producing copious amounts of rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Carlsbad, NM Police Department reported high water across southern portions of the city.  Several streets were impassable, with the water covering the hood of one car.  Most streets within southern Carlsbad were blockaded by law enforcement.

Curb deep water was reported across many areas across the state, though more substantial flooding was noted around Acoma.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture combined with a weak upper level disturbance allowed slow moving thunderstorms to develop across central and western New Mexico.  Torrential rainfall affected many areas, including Gallup, Albuquerque, Los Lunas, and Jemez Springs, where localized flash flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant amounts of water flowed over Thompson Bridge on Telstar Road in Church Rock which eventually caused the bridge to collapse. The bridge served as the only way in and out of the Church Rock community.  Other portions of this road were covered in up to 3 feet of mud.  Challenger Road, which leads up to the elementary school, was covered with up to 5 feet of mud.   Superman Canyon Road, Old Church Rock Coal Road and Becenti Trail were also washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture combined with an upper level wave sliding north across western New Mexico produced more torrential rainfall and repeat flooding for areas around Gallup and the Jemez Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After the Church Rock bridge at Telstar Road collapsed July 31st, a temporary bridge was put in place.  Just 2 days later, another bout of flash flooding resulted in the temporary bridge being washed out. A female flood victim had to be rescued the high waters in Church Rock.  She sustained only minor injuries. Other vehicles were flooded out in Church Rock as well. In addition, mud as deep as 5 feet plagued Challenger Road again. Superman Canyon Road, Old Church Rock Coal Road, and Becenti Trail were also washed out for the 2nd time in as many days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture combined with an upper level wave sliding north across western New Mexico produced more torrential rainfall and repeat flooding for areas around Gallup and the Jemez Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sweetwater, Iyanbito and Dakota Loop Roads were washed out due to flash flooding in Iyanbito for the second time in two days.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A back door cold front delivered even more moisture to eastern New Mexico, resulting in thunderstorms producing heavy rain and high wind gusts, along with penny size hail.

Deep monsoon moisture combined with an easterly wave over southeast New Mexico to create widespread heavy rain across northern and central New Mexico. Hardest hit areas were in the vicinity of the central mountain chain, where several flash floods were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At Ranchitos and Eubank, the shoulders of the roads were eroded with a foot of water covering the intersection.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoon moisture combined with an easterly wave over southeast New Mexico to create widespread heavy rain across northern and central New Mexico. Hardest hit areas were in the vicinity of the central mountain chain, where several flash floods were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water accumulated on State Highway 41 with county roads along side the highway impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Continued deep monsoon moisture led to heavy rain east of San Antonio, with damage to several county roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bosquecito Road was closed due to high, fast moving water just north of U.S. Highway 380 near an arroyo crossing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture combined with a weak upper level disturbance allowed slow moving thunderstorms to develop across central and western New Mexico.  Torrential rainfall affected many areas, including Gallup, Albuquerque, Los Lunas, and Jemez Springs, where localized flash flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to four feet of water was reported near the intersection of Coors and Sage as well as Coors and Tower. Several vehicles were stuck. Knee deep water was approaching structures near Gonzales and 52nd Street.  Three feet of water was also reported at Coors and Bridge with roads closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large complex of showers and thunderstorms slowly moved eastward across the eastern plains of New Mexico during the early morning hours of the 16th.  Rainfall amounts around 2 inches were common, with isolated areas receiving up to 5 inches.  The flooding closed many roadways around Roswell and Portales.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous road were closed or impassible in and near Roswell due to the heavy rains and flooding.  This includes Poe Street west from Southeast Main as well as Brown Road from Country Club to Pine Lodge.  Cars were also reported to have been floating down Highway 285 north of Roswell. Two feet of water ran over the highway between mile markers 136 and 138, leading the road to be closed. A couple of cars ended up in the ditch, with water up to their windshields.  Also along Pine Lodge Road near mile marker 59, three to four feet of water ran over the road where it crossed Salt Creek.  Cricket Creek as well as Spring and Hondo Rivers were running bank full.  Unofficial rainfall reports include near 5 inches near Berrendo Road and North Main, 3.5 inches near the Roswell High School on West Church Street, and 3.0 inches at the 2600 block of South Sunset. Hundreds of homes were without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture combined with a weak upper level disturbance allowed slow moving thunderstorms to develop across central and western New Mexico.  Torrential rainfall affected many areas, including Gallup, Albuquerque, Los Lunas, and Jemez Springs, where localized flash flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cerrillos Rd. between St. Michael's Dr. and Camino Carlos Rey was flooded and impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture combined with a weak upper level disturbance allowed slow moving thunderstorms to develop across central and western New Mexico.  Torrential rainfall affected many areas, including Gallup, Albuquerque, Los Lunas, and Jemez Springs, where localized flash flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused an awning at the Giant gas station to collapse and fall on three cars at the intersection of Sawmill Rd. and St. Francis Dr.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled over the eastern plains, along with an approaching trough, brought moisture, lift and instability to far eastern New Mexico on the 12th. Numerous large hail was observed along with a few high wind reports.

Deep monsoon moisture produced thunderstorms with heavy rain over much of New Mexico. A couple of storms dumped heavy rain over the Magdalena Observatory and the Langmuir Laboratory for Atmospheric Research, resulting in the dirt road leading to the facilities being washed out. Meanwhile outflow boundaries over Quay County produced strong wind gusts and wind damage around Logan and Nara Visa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A SchoolNet site in the Pueblo Point subdivision recorded a 59 mph wind gust.  Report relayed by KVII in Amarillo.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large three to four foot diameter tree trunk was split apart at a height of eight feet off the ground as seen via webcam. Wind gust recorded by SchoolNet site, and relayed by KVII in Amarillo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A back door cold front brought a fresh supply of low level moisture to eastern New Mexico on the 6th. The added moisture and instability produced scattered showers and thunderstorms over the east, with a few of the storms turning severe in Chaves and San Miguel counties.

A back door cold front brought a fresh supply of low level moisture to eastern New Mexico on the 6th. The added moisture and instability produced scattered showers and thunderstorms over the east, with a few of the storms turning severe in Chaves and San Miguel counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limbs were downed by the large hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: One inch to 1.90 inch hail broke three windows out of a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another back door front dropped south across the eastern plains, setting up a boundary for thunderstorms to form. A few of the storms turned severe in Curry and Quay counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind pushed a small Chevy truck, that was in neutral, 30 yards down a driveway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled over the eastern plains, along with an approaching trough, brought moisture, lift and instability to far eastern New Mexico on the 12th. Numerous large hail was observed along with a few high wind reports.

Thunderstorms developed along and just east of the central mountain chain along a moisture boundary during the mid afternoon hours.  Thunderstorms slowly moved toward the east and into an area where better moisture and instability existed. Hail up to the size of golfballs were reported on multiple storms.  The hail broke out windshields on some vehicles.

During the afternoon of the 25th, a sharp dryline draped along the east central plains interacted with upper level energy ejecting east out of Arizona.  The combination of strong instability along the dryline and the upper level disturbance produced widespread strong to severe thunderstorms over Curry and Roosevelt Counties.  A few storms continued into the early morning hours of the 26th.

With abundant low level moisture in place just east of the dryline, afternoon heating led to the development of scattered thunderstorms over Eddy County.  These storms continued into the evening hours, with some storms reaching severe levels.

Deep moisture combined with a weak upper level disturbance allowed slow moving thunderstorms to develop across central and western New Mexico.  Torrential rainfall affected many areas, including Gallup, Albuquerque, Los Lunas, and Jemez Springs, where localized flash flooding was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Small boulders, rocks, and water washed over Highway 4.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoon moisture produced thunderstorms with heavy rain over much of New Mexico. A couple of storms dumped heavy rain over the Magdalena Observatory and the Langmuir Laboratory for Atmospheric Research, resulting in the dirt road leading to the facilities being washed out. Meanwhile outflow boundaries over Quay County produced strong wind gusts and wind damage around Logan and Nara Visa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large landslide washed out the dirt road leading to the Magdalena Ridge Observatory and Langmuir Laboratory for Atmospheric Research.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A microburst resulted in winds upwards of 65 mph near Espanola.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds of 65 mph from a microburst uprooted several small dead trees, and downed a 10 inch diameter green elm tree which damaged parked vehicles.  In addition, several metal roof panels were ripped off an out building.  This located only received a few raindrops, but the spotter saw the storm to the northwest.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lone severe thunderstorm developed near San Felipe Pueblo and moved east-southeast along the east mountains.  Hail up to 2 inches in diameter fell and devastated trees, roofs, windshields and windows across the area.

A back door cold front brought a fresh supply of low level moisture to eastern New Mexico on the 6th. The added moisture and instability produced scattered showers and thunderstorms over the east, with a few of the storms turning severe in Chaves and San Miguel counties.
Deep monsoon moisture produced heavy rain across the Albuquerque Metro area, causing a large Cottonwood tree in southwest Albuquerque to topple over due to the rain-softened ground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large cottonwood tree in southwest Albuquerque was uprooted due to the rain-softened ground. The tree blocked a street and tore up some sidewalk.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Over 1.5 inches of rain fell in 20 minutes southeast of Espanola.  Cars were swept away due to the high waters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 76 was closed due to flash flooding in the vicinity of La Puebla, southeast of Espanola.   Four to five feet of rock and silt was washed over the road.  Cars were washed into nearby arroyos.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist environment was in place under an upper level ridge. A stationary boundary was draped across southern New Mexico providing the lift for slow moving thunderstorms which caused numerous reports of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flooding along Poverty Creek, adjacent properties and low water crossings was reported by a trained spotter.
